questions.question: Match each regularization technique to what it does.
categories: Drives some weights to exactly zero
Shrinks all weights toward zero
Randomly disables neurons during training
Stops training when validation loss increases

correct_mapping.L1 regularization: Drives some weights to exactly zero
correct_mapping.Early stopping: Stops training when validation loss increases

questions.explanation: Each technique prevents overfitting differently: L1 creates sparse models by zeroing out weights. L2 penalizes large weights by shrinking them. Dropout randomly masks neurons to prevent co-adaptation. Early stopping limits how long the model trains.

questions.question: Match each problem to the correct description.
categories: Model memorizes training data, fails on new data
Model too simple, poor on all data
Model makes overly simplistic assumptions
Model is too sensitive to training data

correct_mapping.Overfitting: Model memorizes training data, fails on new data
correct_mapping.Underfitting: Model too simple, poor on all data
correct_mapping.High variance: Model is too sensitive to training data

questions.explanation: Overfitting means the model has learned the training data too well, including noise. Underfitting means the model is too simple. High bias is the cause of underfitting (overly simple assumptions). High variance is the cause of overfitting (too sensitive to specific training examples).

questions.question: Match each data augmentation technique to its transformation.
categories: Mirrors the image left to right
Turns the image by a random angle
Changes brightness, contrast, or saturation
Takes a random portion of the image

correct_mapping.Horizontal flipping: Mirrors the image left to right
correct_mapping.Random rotation: Turns the image by a random angle
correct_mapping.Random cropping: Takes a random portion of the image

questions.explanation: Each augmentation technique creates a different variation of the original image while keeping the same label. Flipping creates mirror images, rotation changes orientation, color jitter varies lighting conditions, and cropping changes what portion of the scene is visible.

questions.question: Match each scenario to the most likely diagnosis.
items: High train accuracy, low test accuracy
Low train accuracy, low test accuracy
High train accuracy, high test accuracy
Train accuracy improves but test accuracy drops
categories: Overfitting
Underfitting
Good generalization
Model is starting to overfit
questions.explanation: The gap between training and test performance reveals the problem. A large gap (high train, low test) means overfitting. Both being low means underfitting. Both being high means good generalization. When test starts dropping while training improves, overfitting is beginning.

questions.question: Match each term to its role in the bias-variance tradeoff.
categories: Error from a model being too simple
Error from a model being too sensitive to data
Technique to reduce variance at the cost of slight bias increase
Increasing it reduces bias but increases variance

correct_mapping.Bias: Error from a model being too simple
correct_mapping.Variance: Error from a model being too sensitive to data
correct_mapping.Regularization: Technique to reduce variance at the cost of slight bias increase
correct_mapping.Model complexity: Increasing it reduces bias but increases variance

questions.explanation: Bias is the error from overly simple assumptions. Variance is the error from being too sensitive to training data. Regularization trades a small increase in bias for a larger decrease in variance. Increasing model complexity reduces bias but increases variance, which is the core tradeoff.

questions.question: Match each regularization technique to where it operates.
categories: Transforms input data
Adds a term to the loss function
Modifies hidden layer activations
Controls the training loop

correct_mapping.L2 weight penalty: Adds a term to the loss function

questions.explanation: Data augmentation works at the input level by transforming images. L2 adds a penalty to the loss function to discourage large weights. Dropout operates on hidden layers by zeroing out neurons. Early stopping controls when training ends by monitoring validation performance.

questions.question: Match each regularization strength setting to its effect.
categories: Minimal effect, model may still overfit
Good balance between fitting and generalization
Model becomes too constrained, underfits
Model has full capacity, highest overfitting risk

correct_mapping.Very low regularization: Minimal effect, model may still overfit
correct_mapping.Moderate regularization: Good balance between fitting and generalization
correct_mapping.No regularization: Model has full capacity, highest overfitting risk

questions.explanation: Regularization strength controls how much the model is constrained. No regularization allows full capacity and maximum overfitting risk. Very low has minimal effect. Moderate achieves the best balance. Very high over-constrains the model, leading to underfitting.

questions.question: Match each concept to its correct definition.
categories: How well the model fits the data it learned from
How well the model performs on held-out data during training
The ability to perform well on new, unseen data
Techniques to prevent overfitting

correct_mapping.Training accuracy: How well the model fits the data it learned from
correct_mapping.Validation accuracy: How well the model performs on held-out data during training
correct_mapping.Generalization: The ability to perform well on new, unseen data

questions.explanation: Training accuracy measures performance on data the model trained on. Validation accuracy measures performance on data set aside during training to monitor progress. Generalization is the overall goal of performing well on any new data. Regularization is the set of techniques used to improve generalization.

questions.question: Match each dropout concept to its description.
categories: Neurons are randomly set to zero
All neurons are active, dropout is off
Neurons relying too heavily on specific other neurons
The probability that a neuron is turned off

correct_mapping.Dropout during training: Neurons are randomly set to zero
correct_mapping.Dropout during testing: All neurons are active, dropout is off
correct_mapping.Co-adaptation: Neurons relying too heavily on specific other neurons
correct_mapping.Dropout rate: The probability that a neuron is turned off

questions.explanation: During training, dropout randomly zeroes out neurons. During testing, dropout is turned off so all neurons contribute. Co-adaptation is the problem dropout solves, where neurons become dependent on each other. The dropout rate is the hyperparameter controlling what fraction of neurons are dropped.

questions.question: Match each technique to the problem it best addresses.
categories: Small training dataset
Weights growing too large
Neurons co-adapting during training
Unstable or slow training
questions.explanation: Data augmentation helps when the dataset is small by creating more diverse examples. L2 regularization directly penalizes large weights. Dropout breaks co-adaptation by randomly removing neurons. Batch normalization stabilizes and speeds up training by normalizing layer inputs.
